title: CSP connect-src omits www.gstatic.com → DRACO decoder fetch blocked, breaks 3D models in production
detail: connect-src lists 'https://fonts.gstatic.com' but NOT 'https://www.gstatic.com'. src/utils/dracoLoader.ts:33 sets DRACO_DECODER_PATH = 'https://www.gstatic.com/draco/versioned/decoders/1.5.7/' and useDracoGLTF() is called at runtime by src/components/models/WorkerModel.tsx:46 and ForkliftModel.tsx:48. Three's DRACOLoader._loadLibrary (node_modules/three/.../DRACOLoader.js:331-368) fetches draco_decoder.js/wasm via THREE.FileLoader (fetch/XHR — governed by connect-src). CSP host-source matching is per-host: 'fonts.gstatic.com' does NOT match 'www.gstatic.com'. forklift.glb is 1.6MB (vs forklift-original.glb at 36MB) = DRACO-compressed, so the decoder fetch fires and is blocked under the enforced CSP, leaving DRACO GLBs unrenderable. Tests are green because they never run under an enforced CSP in a browser. The SW (public/sw.js:142) already allowlists 'gstatic.com/draco' for caching, confirming intent — only the CSP was missed.
fix: Add 'https://www.gstatic.com' to the connect-src directive in index.html (line 11), e.g. '...generativelanguage.googleapis.com https://www.gstatic.com https://cdn.jsdelivr.net...'. Alternatively switch dracoLoader.ts to the local '/draco/' decoder path (commented at line 36) and vendor the decoder into public/draco/ to avoid any third-party connect entirely.
_area: L2 Security · CSP + service worker + dependencies

title: connect-src allows wildcard ws:/wss: to any host
detail: connect-src is 'self' ws: wss: https://generativelanguage.googleapis.com https://cdn.jsdelivr.net https://fonts.gstatic.com https://fonts.googleapis.com. Bare 'ws:' and 'wss:' permit WebSocket connections to ANY origin. With PeerJS (src/multiplayer/SignalingService.ts:26-30) using the default public cloud server and untrusted WebRTC peers, this lets injected/compromised script (or a malicious peer-delivered payload) exfiltrate to arbitrary WS endpoints. The only WS endpoint actually needed is the PeerJS cloud signaler (0.peerjs.com over wss) plus any self-hosted SCADA WS.
fix: Replace bare 'ws: wss:' with the specific signaling host, e.g. 'wss://0.peerjs.com' (and the user's SCADA host if applicable). If a self-hosted PeerServer is later used, add that exact host. Keep 'ws:' only if local-dev SCADA over plain ws is required, scoped to localhost.
_area: L2 Security · CSP + service worker + dependencies

title: cdn.jsdelivr.net in connect-src enables remote font-data fetch from third party (supply-chain surface)
detail: connect-src includes 'https://cdn.jsdelivr.net'. troika-three-text (bundled via @react-three/drei/three-fiber) fetches Unicode font-resolver data at runtime from 'https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/gh/lojjic/unicode-font-resolver@v1.0.1/packages/data' (seen verbatim in built bundle public/v0.20/assets/three-fiber-*.js and the dev module). This is connect-src data (not script-src remote code), so it cannot execute arbitrary JS, but it makes glyph rendering depend on a third-party CDN and adds an exfiltration-eligible host to the policy. If jsdelivr is compromised it can serve crafted JSON, which is parsed but not executed.
fix: Acceptable to keep if 3D text glyph coverage for non-Latin scripts is needed. To eliminate the dependency, self-host the unicode-font-resolver data and pass a local dataUrl to troika's font resolver (or restrict to the Latin subset shipped locally), then remove cdn.jsdelivr.net from connect-src. Product decision on whether non-Latin glyph coverage is required.
_area: L2 Security · CSP + service worker + dependencies

title: CSP missing object-src, base-uri, and frame-ancestors hardening directives
detail: The CSP meta tag declares default-src/script-src/style-src/font-src/img-src/connect-src/worker-src but omits 'object-src', 'base-uri', and 'frame-ancestors'. Without 'object-src none', plugin/embed vectors are not explicitly denied (default-src 'self' covers it but explicit is best-practice for XSS hardening). Without 'base-uri self', an injected <base> tag can hijack all relative URLs (the app relies heavily on relative paths — import.meta.env.BASE_URL, sw.js relative precache). frame-ancestors cannot be set via meta tag (it is ignored in meta and only works as an HTTP header), so clickjacking protection is absent on GitHub Pages which sends no X-Frame-Options/frame-ancestors header.
fix: Add 'object-src 'none';' and 'base-uri 'self';' to the CSP meta in index.html. For frame-ancestors/clickjacking, since GitHub Pages cannot set custom response headers, either accept the residual clickjacking risk (low for a single-page simulator with no auth/sensitive actions) or front the site with a CDN/proxy (e.g. Cloudflare) that injects 'Content-Security-Policy: frame-ancestors 'self'' and 'X-Frame-Options: SAMEORIGIN'. Document the limitation.
_area: L2 Security · CSP + service worker + dependencies

title: Service worker uses skipWaiting()+clients.claim() unconditionally → mixed-version asset serving on deploy
detail: install handler calls self.skipWaiting() (line 100) and activate calls self.clients.claim() (line 125) unconditionally on every new SW. Combined with cache-first for hashed JS/CSS (shouldCacheFirst, lines 67-72) and main.tsx's onUpdate handler being a no-op (src/main.tsx:64-66, empty body — no reload prompt), a user mid-session can have the new SW take control and begin serving NEW index.html (network-first) while the page still references OLD hashed chunks that are gone from the server but may or may not be in cache — producing 'failed to fetch dynamically imported module' / blank-screen on deploy. The CACHE_VERSION bump (line 21) only purges caches on activate; it does not coordinate the in-flight page's chunk references.
fix: Gate skipWaiting behind an explicit SKIP_WAITING postMessage triggered only after the user accepts an update prompt, and implement the onUpdate handler in main.tsx to surface a 'new version available — reload' toast that posts SKIP_WAITING then reloads. This is a UX/behavior change so it needs owner sign-off; minimum safe step is to keep skipWaiting but make onUpdate force a one-time location.reload() after activation to avoid mixed-chunk states.
_area: L2 Security · CSP + service worker + dependencies

title: network-first caches ALL 200 responses including HTML/dynamic, risking stale-serve of stale SPA shell
detail: networkFirst() caches every cacheable (status===200) response into STATIC_CACHE (lines 204-206), including HTML and any non-asset GET. On a later network failure it serves the cached HTML (lines 213-221) which may reference hashed chunks already purged by a CACHE_VERSION bump, and there is no max-age / staleness check. isCacheable() (line 79-81) only checks status===200 with no Cache-Control or content-type gating, so error pages or redirected 200s also get cached. For a digital-twin sim this is mainly an availability/stale-shell issue rather than data integrity, but it can pin users to a broken old shell after a deploy.
fix: In networkFirst, only cache navigations/HTML into a dedicated short-lived cache and skip caching responses whose Cache-Control is no-store, or restrict network-first caching to known same-origin document/manifest requests. Add a Date-stamp check or rely on CACHE_VERSION + the SW update flow above. Behavior change → owner review.
_area: L2 Security · CSP + service worker + dependencies

title: SPA navigation fallback hard-codes absolute '/index.html', breaking under non-root BASE_URL deploys
detail: networkFirst navigation fallback does caches.match('/index.html') (line 220), an absolute root path. The app explicitly supports non-root base URLs (sw.js:27 precache uses './' relative paths, registration scope uses import.meta.env.BASE_URL, and prior deploys live at /v0.10/ and /v0.20/). Under a sub-path deployment the offline navigation fallback would miss the precached './index.html' (cached under the base-relative key) and return the 503 'Offline' response instead of the SPA shell. Also line 146 dev-guard 'url.includes("/@")' is broad and could accidentally bypass legitimate same-origin asset URLs containing '/@' (e.g. scoped paths).
fix: Replace the absolute '/index.html' fallback with a base-relative lookup, e.g. resolve against self.registration.scope or new URL('./index.html', self.location.href). Tighten the '/@' dev guard to '/@vite' / '/@react-refresh' specifics. Low risk; current production base is '/' so impact is latent.
_area: L2 Security · CSP + service worker + dependencies

title: script-src 'unsafe-inline' is broader than needed and is silently negated by the absence of nonces/hashes
detail: script-src is ''self' 'unsafe-inline' 'unsafe-eval' blob:'. 'unsafe-eval' is genuinely required (Rapier/Basis-transcoder WASM compilation via blob workers, troika). However 'unsafe-inline' permits arbitrary inline <script>, which is the classic XSS amplifier: any injected markup that reaches the DOM can execute. The only inline script-like content in index.html is the module loader <script type=module src> (external, not inline) and an inline <style> (covered by style-src, not script-src), so 'unsafe-inline' for scripts appears unnecessary for first-party code. Note: adding a nonce/hash would itself cause browsers to ignore 'unsafe-inline', so the current value provides no protection while widening the attack surface.
fix: Attempt removing 'unsafe-inline' from script-src and verify the build still runs (R3F/Vite emit external modules, not inline scripts). If a bundler-injected inline bootstrap exists, switch to a hashed/nonce'd inline script instead. Keep 'unsafe-eval' and 'blob:' (WASM requirement). Verify in a CSP-enforced browser before shipping → flag for owner since it touches runtime behavior.
_area: L2 Security · CSP + service worker + dependencies

title: PeerJS 1.5.5 + default public signaling server: untrusted-peer trust boundary unhardened
detail: peerjs ^1.5.5 (package.json:38, resolved 1.5.5) connects via the default public PeerJS cloud server (src/multiplayer/SignalingService.ts:26-30) with predictable host peer IDs derived from the room code ('millos-${roomCode}', SignalingService.ts:53-55). Predictable IDs let any third party on the public broker impersonate or pre-claim a host ID / squat room codes, and the broad 'wss:' CSP (see separate finding) does nothing to constrain the broker. This is an inherent property of using the shared public broker with deterministic IDs, not a code bug, but it is a real multiplayer trust-boundary weakness for a feature that explicitly accepts untrusted peers.
fix: Add entropy to host peer IDs (append a random nonce shared out-of-band with the room code) so IDs are unguessable, and/or self-host peerjs-server (referenced at SignalingService.ts:28) with the connect-src locked to that host. Validate/authorize incoming DataConnections at the application layer before trusting peer-sent state. Product decision (changes multiplayer UX/join flow).
_area: L2 Security · CSP + service worker + dependencies

title: DRACO JS decoder executed as third-party code from www.gstatic.com (remote-code dependency)
detail: DRACO_DECODER_PATH = 'https://www.gstatic.com/draco/versioned/decoders/1.5.7/' (line 33) with setDecoderConfig({ type: 'js' }) (line 48). Three's DRACOLoader fetches draco_decoder.js / draco_wasm_wrapper.js and runs them inside a blob Worker. Although the Worker runs from a blob (allowed by worker-src 'self' blob:), the executed code is fetched at runtime from a Google-controlled CDN and is not subresource-integrity-pinned. A compromise or version change at gstatic would execute attacker-controlled JS in the worker context. This is a deliberate trade-off (offline-fallback path to '/draco/' is commented at line 36), but for launch it pins integrity to Google's CDN with no SRI.
fix: Vendor the DRACO 1.5.7 decoder into public/draco/ and switch DRACO_DECODER_PATH to '/draco/' (the commented local path), removing the runtime third-party code fetch entirely and fixing the CSP gap in the same stroke. This also makes DRACO models work offline via the existing SW MODEL_CACHE. Behavior-neutral if the local decoder matches the bundled three version.
_area: L2 Security · CSP + service worker + dependencies

title: SW message handler trusts any message origin/shape (no source/type validation)
detail: In public/sw.js the message handler (lines 231-247) destructures event.data.type without verifying event.origin or that event.data is an object, and replies on event.ports[0] for CLEAR_CACHE and GET_CACHE_SIZE. Any script in any client controlled by this SW (same-origin only, so impact is bounded) can clear all millos- caches or enumerate cached URL pathnames (getCacheStats returns full URL pathnames, lines 259-262). For a same-origin-only app this is low severity, but a successful same-origin XSS gains a cache-wipe / cache-enumeration primitive for free.
fix: Guard the handler: 'if (!event.data || typeof event.data !== "object") return;' and validate type against an allowlist before acting. Optionally restrict GET_CACHE_SIZE detail (drop full pathnames, return counts only). Low-risk hardening, no UX change.
_area: L2 Security · CSP + service worker + dependencies

title: CSP delivered only via meta tag (no enforcement of frame-ancestors/Report-Only/upgrade-insecure)
detail: The entire security policy ships as <meta http-equiv> tags (CSP line 5, X-Content-Type-Options line 14, referrer line 15). Meta-delivered CSP is parsed only after the parser reaches it and silently ignores frame-ancestors, sandbox, and report-uri/report-to directives. On GitHub Pages there is no way to add HTTP response headers, so X-Frame-Options, Strict-Transport-Security, and frame-ancestors are entirely absent. Combined with 'connect-src ws: wss:' wildcard, the policy is weaker than it appears.
fix: Document that GitHub Pages cannot serve security headers and decide whether to front millos.net with a header-injecting CDN (Cloudflare/Netlify) to add HSTS + frame-ancestors + a header-delivered CSP. If staying on GH Pages, keep the meta CSP but acknowledge frame-ancestors/HSTS gaps in the launch risk register. Product/infra decision.
_area: L2 Security · CSP + service worker + dependencies

title: PeerJS debug uses process.env.NODE_ENV in a Vite/browser context (likely undefined → never debug, minor)
detail: PEERJS_CONFIG.debug = process.env.NODE_ENV === 'development' ? 2 : 0 (line 29). Vite exposes import.meta.env, not process.env, in browser code; process.env.NODE_ENV is statically replaced by Vite's define only if configured, otherwise it is undefined at runtime. Practically this means debug is always 0 (the safe branch), so there is no information-leak risk — but it is a latent correctness smell indicating the env check does not do what it reads as, and if a future define exposes process.env in prod it could flip verbose PeerJS logging on. Not a security defect today; flagged for the env-API mismatch.
fix: Use import.meta.env.DEV (consistent with serviceWorkerRegistration.ts:45) instead of process.env.NODE_ENV for the debug gate. Behavior-neutral today, removes the latent footgun.
_area: L2 Security · CSP + service worker + dependencies
